scenes.title: Conditional Probability & Bayes' Rule
scenes.description: Build intuition for conditional probability from joint distributions and areas — then apply Bayes' rule to three real-world problems.

views.name: Side
views.description: See the Z-layers from the side

scenes.markdown:
# Conditional Probability & Bayes' Rule

## The Probability Rectangle

Represent the joint distribution of two binary events as a **unit square** (area = 1):

- **Column width** = $P(X)$ — marginal probability of $X$
- **Row height within each column** = $P(Y \mid X)$ — conditional probability of $Y$ given $X$
- **Cell area** = $P(X) \times P(Y \mid X) = P(X, Y)$

This layout correctly encodes the full joint distribution in the four cells:

$$\text{Area of cell} = P(X, Y)$$

## Independence Is a Straight Line

When $X$ and $Y$ are **independent**, $P(Y \mid X = 0) = P(Y \mid X = 1) = P(Y)$. The horizontal divider between rows is the **same height in every column** — a perfectly straight line.

When they are **dependent**, the divider is **stepped** — different heights in different columns. The size of that step measures the strength of dependence.

## Conditional Probability

Conditioning on $B$ means focusing on just the $B$-row. Within that row, $P(A \mid B)$ is the fraction belonging to the $A$ column:

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)} = \frac{P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A)}{P(B)}$$

Geometrically: **zoom into the B-row**, stretching it to fill the full square. The fraction occupied by the $A$ column in that zoomed view is $P(A \mid B)$.

## Bayes' Rule

From the symmetry of the joint probability:
$$P(A, B) = P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)$$

$$\boxed{P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}}$$

**Belief update:** prior $P(A)$ → observe $B$ → posterior $P(A \mid B)$.

## Why It Surprises Everyone

Three examples where naive intuition fails due to **base rate neglect**:

| Scenario | Naive guess | Actual answer |
|----------|------------|---------------|
| Medical test (0.1% disease, 99% accurate) | ~99% | **~4.7%** |
| Spam filter ("Nigeria" keyword) | high | **~73.8%** |
| Tornado radar (2% base rate, 95% detection) | ~95% | **~39.3%** |

## Key Formulas

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}$$

$$P(B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A) + P(B \mid \overline{A}) \cdot P(\overline{A})$$

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A) + P(B \mid \overline{A}) \cdot P(\overline{A})}$$

steps.title: The Probability Rectangle
steps.description:
The **unit square** represents all possible outcomes (total probability = 1). We split it into two **columns** — one for event $A$, one for $\overline{A}$ — where the **column width equals the probability**.

Adjust $P(A)$ to see the column split change. This is the first axis of the joint distribution.

info.content:
### Column widths = P(X)
$P(A) = {{pA}}$
$P(\overline{A}) = {{toFixed(1-pA,2)}}$
---
Column width encodes
marginal probability.
Area of left column = $P(A) = {{pA}}$
Area of right column = ${{toFixed(1-pA,2)}}$

steps.title: The Joint Distribution
steps.description:
Now add event $B$ as a **row split within each column**. The height of the $B$-row in each column is the **conditional probability** $P(B \mid \text{column})$.

Crucially, $P(B \mid A)$ and $P(B \mid \overline{A})$ can be **different** — that's dependence. Each cell's area equals the joint probability $P(X, Y)$.

**Independence**: set both $P(B \mid A)$ and $P(B \mid \overline{A})$ to the same value — the horizontal divider becomes a straight line.

sliders.label: $P(B \mid A)$ — row height in A column

info.content:
### Joint Distribution
Cell area = $P(X,Y)$
---
$P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A)$
$\quad = {{pA}} \times {{pBgA}} = {{toFixed(pA*pBgA,3)}}$
$P(\overline{A} \cap B) = {{toFixed((1-pA)*pBgNA,3)}}$
$P(B) = {{toFixed(pA*pBgA+(1-pA)*pBgNA,3)}}$
---
**Independent?**
$P(B \mid A) = {{pBgA}}$
$P(B \mid \overline{A}) = {{pBgNA}}$
{{abs(pBgA-pBgNA)<0.02 ? 'Yes — step line is horizontal' : 'No — step line is off-horizontal'}}

steps.title: Independence Is a Straight Line
steps.description:
**Independence** means knowing $A$ tells you nothing about $B$: $P(B \mid A) = P(B \mid \overline{A})$.

The **bar chart to the right** shows P(B|A) and P(B|¬A) side by side. When they match, the step line in the rectangle is a straight horizontal line — that is independence.

**Dependence** creates mismatched bars and a step. Set both sliders to the same value to see the bars align and the step disappear.

info.content:
### Independence
$P(B \mid A) = {{pBgA}}$
$P(B \mid \overline{A}) = {{pBgNA}}$
$P(B) = {{toFixed(pA*pBgA+(1-pA)*pBgNA,3)}}$
---
Step height:
$|P(B \mid A) - P(B \mid \overline{A})| = {{toFixed(abs(pBgA-pBgNA),3)}}$
---
{{abs(pBgA-pBgNA)<0.02 ? '✓ Independent — straight line' : pBgA>pBgNA ? 'B more likely when A occurs' : 'B less likely when A occurs'}}

steps.title: Conditioning on B: Zoom In
steps.description:
**Conditioning on B** means restricting attention to just the $B$-row — the bottom strip. We zoom in on it, stretching it to fill the entire square.

In the zoomed view (elevated at z = 2.5), the $A$ column occupies exactly $P(A \mid B)$ of the width. The **column widths change** when you zoom in, because the B-row has different heights in each column.

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{\text{area of } A \cap B}{\text{area of } B\text{-row}} = \frac{P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A)}{P(B)}$$

Use the **Side** camera view to see the 3D projection clearly.

info.content:
### Conditioning on B
$P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A)}{P(B)}$
---
$P(A \cap B) = {{toFixed(pA*pBgA,3)}}$
$P(B) = {{toFixed(pA*pBgA+(1-pA)*pBgNA,3)}}$
---
$P(A \mid B) = {{toFixed(pA*pBgA/(pA*pBgA+(1-pA)*pBgNA),3)}}$
$P(\overline{A} \mid B) = {{toFixed((1-pA)*pBgNA/(pA*pBgA+(1-pA)*pBgNA),3)}}$
---
Note: $P(A)={{pA}}$ but $P(A \mid B) = {{toFixed(pA*pBgA/(pA*pBgA+(1-pA)*pBgNA),3)}}$
{{abs(pBgA-pBgNA)<0.02 ? 'Same! A and B are independent.' : 'Different — B shifts the belief about A.'}}

steps.title: Conditioning on A: Zoom In
steps.description:
**Conditioning on A** means restricting attention to just the $A$-column — the left strip. We zoom it horizontally to fill the entire square.

In the zoomed view (elevated at z = 2.5), the $B$ row occupies exactly $P(B \mid A)$ of the height. Compare this to the previous slide where we zoomed the B-row.

$$P(B \mid A) = \frac{\text{area of } A \cap B}{\text{area of } A\text{-column}} = \frac{P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A)}{P(A)}$$

Use the **Side** camera view to see both zoom levels.

info.content:
### Conditioning on A
$P(B \mid A) = \dfrac{P(A) \cdot P(B \mid A)}{P(A)}$
---
$P(A \cap B) = {{toFixed(pA*pBgA,3)}}$
$P(A) = {{pA}}$
---
$P(B \mid A) = {{toFixed(pBgA,3)}}$
$P(\overline{B} \mid A) = {{toFixed(1-pBgA,3)}}$
---
Note: $P(B)={{toFixed(pA*pBgA+(1-pA)*pBgNA,3)}}$ but $P(B \mid A)={{pBgA}}$
{{abs(pBgA-(pA*pBgA+(1-pA)*pBgNA))<0.02 ? 'Same! A and B are independent.' : 'Different — A changes the probability of B.'}}

steps.title: Asymmetry: $P(A \mid B) \neq P(B \mid A)$
steps.description:
You have now seen both zoomed views — conditioning on B and conditioning on A.

Same four cells, two different lenses. The intersection area $P(A \cap B)$ is shared, but the denominators differ: $P(B)$ vs $P(A)$. That's why $P(A \mid B) \neq P(B \mid A)$ in general — same intersection, different normalization.

info.content:
### Asymmetry
Same intersection, different denominators:
---
$P(A \cap B) = {{toFixed(pA*pBgA,3)}}$
---
$P(A \mid B) = {{toFixed(pA*pBgA,3)}} / {{toFixed(pA*pBgA+(1-pA)*pBgNA,3)}}$
$\quad\quad\quad\; = {{toFixed(pA*pBgA/(pA*pBgA+(1-pA)*pBgNA),3)}}$

$P(B \mid A) = {{toFixed(pA*pBgA,3)}} / {{pA}}$
$\quad\quad\quad\; = {{toFixed(pBgA,3)}}$
---
{{abs(pA*pBgA/(pA*pBgA+(1-pA)*pBgNA)-pBgA)<0.01 ? 'Equal here (independent)' : 'Unequal — this is the usual case'}}

steps.title: Bayes' Rule
steps.description:
From the symmetry of joint probability:
$$P(A, B) = P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A) = P(A \mid B) \cdot P(B)$$

Rearranging gives **Bayes' rule**:
$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$$

This is the **belief update formula**: start with prior $P(A)$, observe evidence $B$, compute posterior $P(A \mid B)$. The denominator $P(B) = P(B \mid A)P(A) + P(B \mid \overline{A})P(\overline{A})$ just normalizes so probabilities sum to 1.

The three examples next show where intuition fails catastrophically.

info.content:
### Bayes' Rule
$P(A \mid B) = \dfrac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$
---
**Prior** $P(A) = {{pA}}$
**Likelihood** $P(B \mid A) = {{pBgA}}$
**Marginal** $P(B) = {{toFixed(pA*pBgA+(1-pA)*pBgNA,3)}}$
---
**Posterior** $P(A \mid B) = {{toFixed(pA*pBgA/(pA*pBgA+(1-pA)*pBgNA),3)}}$
---
Verify from rectangle:
$A \cap B$ area = ${{toFixed(pA*pBgA,3)}}$
$B$-row area = ${{toFixed(pA*pBgA+(1-pA)*pBgNA,3)}}$
Ratio = ${{toFixed(pA*pBgA/(pA*pBgA+(1-pA)*pBgNA),3)}}$ ✓

steps.title: Example 1 — Medical Testing
steps.description:
A disease affects **1%** of people. A test is **99% sensitive** and has a **2% false positive rate**.

**You test positive. What's the probability you actually have the disease?**

Most people say ~99%. The real answer at 1% prevalence is ~**33%** — still a surprise.

**Left column** (width = prevalence) = disease. **Right column** = no disease. The **bottom row** (below the step line) = tested positive. Green = true positive, orange = false positive. The posterior $P(D \mid +)$ is the green fraction of that bottom row.

Slide prevalence down to **0.1%** to see the famous 4.7% result.

steps.title: Example 2 — Spam Filtering
steps.description:
**15%** of emails are spam. **80%** of spam contains "Nigeria". Only **5%** of legitimate emails contain it.

**An email with "Nigeria" arrives — what's the probability it's spam?**

**Left column** = emails **containing the word** (width = ~16%). **Right column** = no word. Within each column: **orange** = spam, **blue** = legit. The posterior $P(\text{spam} \mid W) \approx 73.8\%$ is the orange fraction in the left column.

info.content:
### Spam Filter
Keyword: "Nigeria"
---
$P(\text{spam}) = {{pSpam}}$
$P(W \mid \text{spam}) = {{pWspam}}$
$P(W \mid \text{legit}) = {{pWleg}}$
---
**Left column width:**
$P(W) = {{toFixed(pSpam*pWspam+(1-pSpam)*pWleg,3)}}$
---
**Per 10,000 emails:**
Spam with word: {{toFixed(pSpam*pWspam*10000,0)}}
Legit with word: {{toFixed((1-pSpam)*pWleg*10000,0)}}
---
$P(\text{spam} \mid W) = {{toFixed(pSpam*pWspam/(pSpam*pWspam+(1-pSpam)*pWleg)*100,1)}}\%$

steps.title: Example 3 — Tornado Warning
steps.description:
Only **2%** of severe storm alerts are real tornadoes. Radar detects **95%** of actual tornadoes, but triggers for **3%** of non-tornado storms.

**The radar fires. What's the probability of an actual tornado?** Answer: ~**39.3%**.

**Left column** (width = 2%) = real tornadoes. **Right column** = non-tornado storms. **Bottom row** = radar fired. Red cell = true alarm, orange = false alarm. The posterior P(T|radar) is the red fraction of the bottom row.

**Try increasing the base rate**: as tornadoes become more common, the posterior climbs rapidly.

scenes.prompt:
You are teaching conditional probability and Bayes' rule using the correct area-based rectangle model.

## The Core Geometric Model (CRITICAL)
The rectangle divides into four cells:
- **Columns** = values of X (A vs ¬A). Column WIDTH = P(X) — marginal probability.
- **Rows within each column** = conditional distribution P(Y|X). Row HEIGHT in each column = P(Y|X).
- **Cell area** = P(X) × P(Y|X) = P(X,Y) — joint probability.
- The dividing line between rows is STEPPED (not horizontal) when dependent.
- Independence means straight horizontal line: P(B|A) = P(B|¬A).

## When Explaining Conditioning
- Conditioning on B = zoom into the B-row (bottom strip)
- The B-row has height pBgA in the A-column and height pBgNA in the ¬A-column
- After zooming: A-column occupies P(A|B) fraction, NOT P(A) fraction
- This is why P(A|B) ≠ P(A) when dependent

## For Real-World Examples
All three examples use the same column/row structure:
- Column width = base rate (P(disease), P(spam), P(tornado))
- Row height in each column = conditional test/signal probability
- The stepped dividing line size = how informative the test is
- Base rate neglect = the left column is tiny, so even a tall green cell has small area

## Exploration Prompts
- 'Set P(B|A) = P(B|¬A) and notice the step disappears — that is what independence looks like'
- 'In the medical example, try prevalence 10% vs 0.1% and see how the posterior swings'
- 'What makes a test useful? Both low FPR and high base rate matter'
- 'Can you find slider values where P(A|B) is actually less than P(A)?'
